title: Blanchardyn and Eglantine
summary: The repository XML is a local version of University of Michigan CME item Blanchardyn, keyed from the 1962 reprint of Leon Kellner's 1890 EETS edition. Kellner transcribed the unique incomplete Caxton print now Manchester Incunable 15027, supplied its missing internal leaf and French comparison text from BnF Français 24371, and used the Huntington's 1595 Goodwine adaptation for parallel specimens, the continuation after the Caxton copy breaks off, and an appendix. Caxton's c. 1489 Middle English work translates and adapts a now-unidentified physical copy of the long Burgundian French prose Blancandin. Complete public IIIF bundles are cached locally for the full 210-canvas Manchester bound volume and all 115 canvases of BnF Français 24371, with exact work ranges recorded inside each full object.
review_notes: The abstract Caxton work, its lost French exemplar, two extant source objects, Goodwine adaptation, Kellner editions, U-M encoding, modern Hudson edition, digital reproductions, and repository artifact are modeled separately.

The Manchester cache contains the complete 210-canvas bound object. Caxton's work is mapped to canvas labels 14–204, while later inserted leaves, blanks, flyleaves, and binding views remain in the full bundle.
The BnF cache contains all 115 canvases of Français 24371. Visual inspection maps the long prose romance to canvases f2–f111 and leaves binding and flyleaf views intact.

Manchester's exact item metadata and provider policy apply CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 to its images; Gallica permits free attributed noncommercial reuse but requires a paid license for commercial reuse. Local caching does not authorize redistribution.

The current U-M item says Public Domain / No Copyright–United States, while the 1997 XML header contains a restrictive legacy availability statement. Both are preserved pending provider clarification.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for Blanchardyn
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The source uses the older DLPSTEXTCLASS family and carries IDNO Blanchardyn.
Its exact upstream release and transfer history remain unresolved.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item Blanchardyn
entities.description: A keyed and structurally marked-up electronic representation of Kellner's composite edition, with parallel print columns serialized by chapter and some back matter omitted.
notes: The XML credits keyboarding to Chrisian, encoding and five-percent proofreading to Kenneth Church, and markup review to Catherine Paul.
The declared LANGUSAGE lists French and Latin but omits the Middle English of Caxton's primary text.

entities.label: Oxford University Press reprint of Kellner's Blanchardyn and Eglantine (1962)
entities.description: The 1962 reprint explicitly named in the U-M source description and represented by the source edition's call number.

bibliographic.citation: Leon Kellner, ed., Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489, EETS Extra Series 58 (London: Oxford University Press, 1962 reprint).
bibliographic.title: Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 58

notes: The source explicitly says ‘FIRST PUBLISHED 1890’ and ‘REPRINTED 1962.’
No separate substantive revision is asserted.

entities.label: Leon Kellner's Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine (1890)
entities.description: A composite scholarly edition of the unique incomplete Caxton print, supplemented from BnF Français 24371 and Thomas Pope Goodwine's 1595 English adaptation, with a long linguistic introduction, notes, appendix, index, and glossary.

bibliographic.citation: Leon Kellner, ed., Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489: From Lord Spencer's Unique Imperfect Copy, Completed by the Original French and the Second English Version of 1595, EETS Extra Series 58 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1890).
bibliographic.title: Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 58

entities.label: William Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, Manchester Incunable 15027
entities.description: The unique known surviving copy of Caxton's c. 1489 print. It is incomplete: the fifth preliminary leaf, the leaf after Bii, and all text after Miiii are missing; later inserted matter includes a handwritten replacement table leaf.

bibliographic.citation: William Caxton, Blanchardyn and Eglantine (Westminster: William Caxton, c. 1489), Manchester, University of Manchester Library, Incunable 15027.
bibliographic.title: Blanchardyn and Eglantine

holding.notes: Part of the University of Manchester Library Early Printing Collection; previously in the Spencer library.

physical_description.extent: 96 surviving original folios in the present bound object
physical_description.format: Folio incunable; single columns, usually 31 lines
notes: Printed in Caxton type 6 with woodcut initials.
The current digital object has 210 canvases because it includes every side of original and inserted leaves plus binding and flyleaves.

entities.label: French prose Blanchardyn exemplar supplied to Caxton by Margaret Beaufort
entities.description: The physical French book that Caxton says he had earlier sold to Margaret Beaufort and that she returned with a request that he translate it into English. Its present identity and location are not established.

date_statements.label: source-version production
date_statements.value: long French prose version composed in the later fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: This dates the source version, not the unidentified physical exemplar.

notes: BnF Français 24371 is kept separate as an extant close witness and Kellner's actual supplement source.
‘Missing’ here means that no present physical identification was established, not that destruction has been proved.

entities.label: BnF Français 24371
alternate_labels: Roman de Blanchandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'amour
entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper manuscript of the long French prose Blancandin. Kellner collated and transcribed it to supply a missing leaf, French parallels, and the ending of the incomplete Caxton print.

notes: Bound in red morocco with Cardinal Richelieu's arms.
The complete Gallica object contains 115 two-page-spread, binding, and flyleaf canvases.

entities.label: Thomas Pope Goodwine's The Moste Pleasaunt Historye of Blanchardine (1595)
entities.description: The substantial Elizabethan refashioning of Caxton's romance. Kellner used the unique surviving 1595 copy for parallel early chapters, the continuation after Caxton's copy ends, and the appendix of title pages and dedications.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Pope Goodwine, The Moste Pleasaunt Historye of Blanchardine (London: Edward Allde for William Blackwall, 1595), Huntington Library, call number 81464.
bibliographic.title: The Moste Pleasaunt Historye of Blanchardine

physical_description.extent: Two parts in one volume; [142] pages

entities.label: Harriet Hudson's METS edition of Blanchardyn and Eglantine (2023/2024)
entities.description: A modern scholarly edition based on Manchester Incunable 15027, with missing narrative supplied by Hudson's English translation of BnF Français 24371 and with textual and explanatory notes.

bibliographic.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., William Caxton's Paris and Vienne and Blanchardyn and Eglantine (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2023; METS digital edition, 2024).
bibliographic.title: William Caxton's Paris and Vienne and Blanchardyn and Eglantine

entities.label: Decaix 2022 study of Goodwine's Blanchardine
entities.description: A peer-reviewed open-access study distinguishing Goodwine's Elizabethan refashioning from Caxton's translation and documenting the 1595 and 1597 prints.

bibliographic.citation: Cécile Decaix, ‘Thomas Pope Goodwine's Most Pleasant History of Blanchadyne (1595): The Silent Revival of a Text by Caxton,’ Shakespeare en devenir 16 (2022).
bibliographic.title: Thomas Pope Goodwine's Most Pleasant History of Blanchadyne (1595): The Silent Revival of a Text by Caxton

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A
entities.description: Official guidance on duration of copyright used only for cautious U.S. term assessments of old published editions.

agents.name: Thomas Pope Goodwine
notes: The 1595 print uses the initials P. T. G.; the expanded name appears in the 1597 dedication, and the person's identity remains uncertain.

scope.notes: The content and identifier match U-M; the exact transfer event and any intervening normalization are not documented.

object.description: Dedication, contents, introduction, edited romance, and appendix

scope.notes: The Name Index and Glossary are omitted; the XML editorial statement says back matter is excluded.
The reprint statement is explicit; no newly revised transcription is evidenced.

object.description: Corresponding passages of the long French prose romance

scope.notes: The edition also uses the manuscript for comparison notes; this edge covers directly printed French transcription.

subject.description: Parallel opening chapters, continuation after Caxton breaks off, and appendix

object.description: Corresponding passages, title pages, and dedications of the 1595 two-part print

scope.notes: Goodwine is a later adaptation, not a witness to Caxton's exact lost wording.

subject.description: Caxton's Middle English translation and adaptation

object.description: The French prose book supplied by Margaret Beaufort

scope.notes: This is a cross-language work-level relationship; it does not claim that the English print is a facsimile or diplomatic transcription of the French exemplar.
The relation records adaptation, not an assertion that every Goodwine passage directly copies the surviving Manchester leaves.

subject.description: Edited Caxton dedication, table, and surviving romance text

scope.notes: Hudson identifies Manchester Incunable 15027 as the base text.

subject.description: Modern English supply for narrative missing from the unique Caxton print

work_portion.label: Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine (unique surviving copy)
locators: IIIF canvas labels 14–204 (image resources page13–page203): Caxton dedication through the surviving end at sig. Miiii verso
surviving print: 96 original folios; the digitized bound object also includes later inserted leaves, blanks, flyleaves, and binding views
notes: Visual inspection places Caxton's dedication at image resource page13 and the last surviving printed page at page203. The later supplied handwritten table leaf appears within this range at page20; the lost leaf after Bii and the lost conclusion after Miiii are not present.

locators: physical manuscript: entire text block, catalogued as 105 folios

rights.basis: The current exact U-M item page says the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: No Copyright–United States is jurisdiction-specific and is not a worldwide dedication.
The embedded 1997 availability statement conflicts with this current presentation and is recorded separately.

rights.component: Legacy availability statement embedded in the 1997 XML
rights.basis: The embedded statement permits personal use, research, teaching, and linking but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
notes: This preserves the historical text of the statement without deciding whether it remains operative after U-M's current public-domain label.

rights.component: Caxton's c. 1489 English text and typography as historical content
rights.basis: The work was printed around 1489 and is far outside any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This does not grant rights in Manchester's modern photographs, catalog prose, or interface.

rights.component: University of Manchester Library digital photographs and IIIF derivatives
rights.basis: The exact item metadata names The University of Manchester Library as image rights holder and applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0; the Library's digital-collections support page repeats that license.
notes: The NonCommercial condition is incompatible with redistributing these images as part of commercial print-on-demand materials without separate permission.
The ignored local cache is for research comparison and must not be copied into a public build merely because it is present locally.

rights.basis: The manuscript text is fifteenth century and far outside any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This does not determine reuse conditions for Gallica's modern digital images.

rights.component: Gallica digital images and complete IIIF surrogate of BnF Français 24371
rights.basis: The exact manifest links Gallica's conditions. Those conditions permit free noncommercial reuse with the prescribed source credit and require authorization and a paid license for commercial reuse.
notes: Copyright status and public-information reuse conditions are separate questions; this record does not call the image files openly licensed.
Commercial publication, including commercial print-on-demand reuse of the images, requires following the BnF declaration and fee process.

rights.component: The 1595 English text and historical print content
rights.basis: The work was published in 1595 and is far outside any U.S. copyright term.
notes: This does not grant access to or rights in EEBO's microfilm-derived images.

rights.component: Kellner's 1890 edition, transcription, introduction, notes, and apparatus
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1890, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Any cover, preliminaries, production matter, or other material newly created for the 1962 reprint
rights.basis: The issue is a 1962 reprint of a public-domain 1890 edition, but the evidence reviewed does not establish whether all 1962 issue-specific matter is unoriginal or separately protected.
notes: This uncertainty does not transfer a new copyright term to Kellner's 1890 text.

rights.component: Hudson's modern edition, translations, notes, and METS digital text
rights.basis: The edition was published in 2023/2024, and METS states that digital editions are for individual use only and that reprinting the digital text requires contacting its Executive Director.
notes: METS separately licenses its XML CC BY-NC-SA 4.0; no exact XML deliverable for this edition was downloaded or modeled here.
The public HTML is a research and comparison source, not reusable replacement text for the repository edition.

rights.component: Decaix's article text and journal presentation
rights.basis: The journal page expressly distributes the open-access article under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 3.0 France license.

editorial_practices.description: Kellner prints the surviving Caxton text, supplies one missing leaf from French, adds parallel early passages from Goodwine 1595, continues the lost ending with Goodwine and French in parallel, and appends Goodwine's title pages and dedications.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited romance and appendix, printed pp. 1–234

editorial_practices.description: Kellner compares Caxton against the long French prose manuscript and marks additions, omissions, French equivalents, and editorial corrections in notes and typography.
editorial_practices.scope: Caxton text and French comparison apparatus

editorial_practices.description: The electronic edition includes the front matter, introduction, edited text, and appendix but omits the back-matter Name Index and Glossary.
Printed parallel Caxton/1595 and English/French versions are serialized chapter by chapter. Bracketed milestone labels were inserted for orientation and did not occur in Kellner's edition.
editorial_practices.scope: Parallel passages at the beginning and ending of the edited romance

editorial_practices.description: XML corr elements represent Kellner's corrections and preserve the source reading in sic attributes; add and note markup distinguishes some editor-identified departures from or supplies against the French.

evidence.citation: Blanchardyn XML editorial declaration and source-boundary notes.

evidence.summary: The declaration documents keying, proofing, exclusions, correction markup, inserted milestones, serialized parallel versions, and source use; notes mark the internal French supply and the exact break in Lord Spencer's Caxton copy.

evidence.citation: Blanchardyn XML dedication, table, and romance body.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY, especially DIV0.70–DIV0.142
evidence.summary: Caxton's dedication states that Margaret Beaufort supplied the French book and requested translation; the table and narrative establish the intended work and its content.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Blanchardyn item information.
evidence.locator: title, editors, publication, rights, contents, and full text
evidence.summary: The exact item page confirms the digital text, bibliographic source, 1890 date, full-web-text access, public-domain assertion, and No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Leon Kellner, ed., Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489, EETS Extra Series 58 (1890), complete scan.
evidence.locator: title page; contents; introduction pp. cx–cxxvi; text pp. 1–223; appendix pp. 225–234
evidence.summary: The scan confirms Kellner's edition, source statements, Caxton-copy lacunae, use of BnF Français 24371 and the 1595 print, selected parallel texts, and the edition's extent and practices.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record for Kellner's 1962 reprint.
evidence.locator: publication, description, first-publication note, contents, and series
evidence.summary: The catalog confirms the 1962 Oxford University Press issue, cxxvi + 242 pages, its status as a reprint first published in 1890, and its composite contents.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Early Printing Collection, Bookreader 15027 / Incunable 15027.
evidence.locator: full item metadata
evidence.summary: The exact record identifies the work, Caxton, [c1489], Westminster, Middle English, physical dimensions, former owners, reference number, standard catalogs, image capture, image rights holder, and CC BY-NC-SA license.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library IIIF Presentation manifest for Incunable 15027.
evidence.locator: complete 210-canvas sequence
evidence.summary: The manifest exposes all 210 ordered canvases and exact IIIF Image API services for the complete bound object.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of all Manchester Incunable 15027 IIIF canvases and representative full-resolution pages.
evidence.locator: image resources page0–page209; work range page13–page203
evidence.summary: Inspection confirms complete binding-to-binding digital coverage and maps the Caxton work from the dedication at page13 through the last surviving text at page203, with the supplied table leaf at page20 and narrative start at page24.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Digital collections help and support.
evidence.locator: About Library Digitised Collections
evidence.summary: The Library states that its digitized-collection images and metadata are normally produced by its Heritage Imaging Team and that images are published under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 for download and reuse under that license.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Français 24371, Roman de Blanchandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'amour.
evidence.locator: title, shelfmark, date, language, support, extent, dimensions, and binding
evidence.summary: The BnF record and attached digital-object metadata identify Français 24371 as a fifteenth-century French paper manuscript of 105 folios measuring 275 × 190 mm.

evidence.citation: Gallica IIIF Presentation manifest for BnF Français 24371.
evidence.locator: metadata, license, and complete 115-canvas sequence
evidence.summary: The manifest identifies the manuscript, links its catalog and Gallica terms, and exposes 115 ordered IIIF image services covering the complete digital object.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of all BnF Français 24371 IIIF canvases through contact sheets and representative full views.
evidence.locator: canvases f1–f115; work range f2–f111
evidence.summary: Inspection confirms full binding-to-binding coverage; the opening and table appear at f2, continuous romance text fills the object, the explicit occurs on the left page of f111, and f1 plus f112–f115 are binding or flyleaf views.

evidence.citation: The Huntington, The moste pleasaunt historye of Blanchardine (1595), call number 81464.
evidence.locator: title, date, call number, dimensions/format, and associated people
evidence.summary: The current item page identifies the surviving 1595 print, call number 81464, quarto format, and associations with Caxton and Goodwine; it states that an image is not available.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record for the EEBO reproduction of Goodwine's 1595 Blanchardine.
evidence.locator: imprint, extent, signatures, adaptation note, holding of reproduced original, and EEBO link
evidence.summary: The record identifies Edward Allde and William Blackwall, [142] pages in two parts, Goodwine's paraphrase of Caxton, the Huntington original, and subscription EEBO reproduction.

evidence.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., William Caxton's Paris and Vienne and Blanchardyn and Eglantine, METS (2023/2024).
evidence.locator: edition details; Introduction to Blanchardyn; Textual Matters; Witnesses and Source Texts
evidence.summary: The current scholarly edition documents the French verse and prose tradition, Caxton's translation and patron, genre, date, base print, physical lacunae, Manchester identifiers, and Hudson's use of BnF Français 24371 for missing narrative.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation.
evidence.locator: Copyright
evidence.summary: METS says its digital editions are for individual use only, reprinting the digital text requires contacting the Executive Director, and METS XML is separately licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Cécile Decaix, ‘Thomas Pope Goodwine's Most Pleasant History of Blanchadyne (1595): The Silent Revival of a Text by Caxton,’ Shakespeare en devenir 16 (2022).
evidence.locator: paragraphs 1–10, conclusion, notes 1–5 and 19, rights statement
evidence.summary: The article identifies Goodwine's 1595 work as a substantial refashioning/paraphrase of Caxton rather than a fresh Latin translation, gives the historical imprints, and is distributed CC BY-NC 3.0 France.
notes: The advertised PDF endpoint timed out during two local retrieval attempts; the public HTML was fully accessible and is the evidence actually used.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: duration tables for works first published in the United States
evidence.summary: Official term guidance supports the cautious U.S. public-domain assessments for the historical prints and 1890 edition and the separate uncertainty assigned to any issue-specific 1962 material.

open_questions.question: Was Caxton's physical French exemplar one of the surviving long-prose manuscripts, and what precise stemmatic relationship does it have to BnF Français 24371?
next_steps: Consult Rosa Anna Greco's 2002 edition and stemma, Hudson's complete textual notes, and manuscript provenance scholarship.
Do not merge the lost exemplar with BnF Français 24371 without codicological provenance rather than textual similarity alone.

open_questions.question: Does U-M's current Public Domain / No Copyright–United States label supersede or otherwise change the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 1997 Blanchardyn XML?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections to reconcile the exact item's current rights statement with the legacy availability text.
Until clarified, preserve both assertions and avoid claiming an unrestricted worldwide license for the encoding or markup.

open_questions.question: What exact U-M release, download date, and transformation history produced the pinned local Blanchardyn XML blob?
next_steps: Inspect older CME repository history and surviving U-M bulk-download documentation.
Record any intervening normalization or identifier transformation when documentary evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Is a complete openly accessible facsimile of Huntington call number 81464 available outside subscription EEBO?
next_steps: Ask the Huntington whether its complete digital surrogate can be made publicly available or supplied for scholarly research.
If a public surrogate appears, cache the complete physical object and record exact image terms rather than extracting only Kellner's cited passages.
notes: The Huntington item page currently says Image not available; EEBO access is subscription based.

open_questions.question: Did the 1962 reprint add any protectable cover, prefatory, editorial, or production matter not present in the public-domain 1890 edition?
next_steps: Compare a complete physical 1962 copy against the cached 1890 scan.
Keep issue-specific matter separate from Kellner's 1890 public-domain text.
